root_motivation: Today the eight wintermute tools and recall compose only through shell + jq, which loses the record structure at each pipe boundary: a `recall query` JSON-with-evidence flattened to ids becomes a re-query nightmare. Apipe is a shared NDJSON record schema plus a thin streaming runtime so each stage of a pipeline enriches records rather than reducing them to bytes. Phase 0 (this slice) ships the runtime binary with `pass`/`top`/`sort`/`pretty`/`schema`/`filter`/`to-paths`/`from-paths` and the shared schema (recall_hit, transcript_turn, file_event); adapter-mode changes to existing tools land in a separate slice.
user_persona: jsy and Claude sessions on this laptop, composing tool output in shell pipelines. Typical invocation: `recall query 'foo' --format apipe | apipe top 5 | apipe pretty`. Consumers: humans reading the pretty table; downstream tools reading NDJSON; jq-style shell scripts via `apipe to-paths`.

acceptance_criteria.description: `apipe pass` reads NDJSON from stdin, writes byte-identical NDJSON to stdout. Identity filter; useful for type-checking a pipeline. Empty input → empty output, exit 0.

Every record must have required fields `kind`, `source`, `id`, `ts`. `apipe pass` (and every other subcommand) validates each input line; missing required fields → write the original line + an error message to stderr with the 1-indexed line number, drop the record, continue processing. Exit 0 if any records passed through; exit 1 if no input was valid.

`apipe top N` reads NDJSON, emits up to N records with the highest `score` (default field). `--by <field>` selects a different numeric field. Stable for ties (preserves input order). Streaming with bounded memory: maintains a min-heap of size N rather than buffering all input.
`apipe sort -k <field>` reads all NDJSON into memory, emits records sorted ascending by `<field>` (numeric if all values are numeric, else lexicographic). `--desc` reverses. Sort is stable.

`apipe schema` (no args) prints the list of known record kinds to stdout, one per line. `apipe schema --kind <name>` prints the JSON Schema for that kind. Unknown kind → exit 2 with stderr diagnostic.

`apipe filter <expr>` keeps records where `<expr>` evaluates true. `<expr>` is a minimal predicate language: `<field> <op> <literal>` with ops `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `~` (substring). String literals quoted; numeric literals bare. Example: `apipe filter 'score > 0.8'`.

`apipe pretty` reads NDJSON, emits a human-readable table to stdout. Columns: kind, id, ts, subject, score, plus a one-line snippet of payload. Width-aware: truncates long fields with `…`. Output is line-oriented but NOT NDJSON; pretty is a sink stage (don't chain after it).

`apipe to-paths` reads NDJSON, emits bare path strings (one per line) extracted from records of kind=file_event (from `payload.path`) or any record with a top-level `payload.path` field. Non-matching records dropped silently. Escape hatch back to shell `xargs`-style usage.

`apipe from-paths` reads bare path strings, emits file_event records with `payload.path` set. Stable id derived from `sha256(path)[..12]`, `ts` set to file mtime if the file exists, else current time.

Malformed JSON lines on input (parse error, not just missing required fields) are reported to stderr with line number, record dropped, processing continues. The pipeline does not abort on a single malformed line.

scope: Rust 2024 CLI crate `agent-pipe` producing an `apipe` binary.
Shared NDJSON record schema with required fields: kind, source, id, ts, plus optional session_id, subject, score, payload, annotations.
Three record kinds in v1: recall_hit, transcript_turn, file_event (each with documented payload sub-schema).
Streaming subcommands: pass, top, filter, to-paths, from-paths, schema (constant memory).
Buffering subcommands: sort, pretty (load-into-memory permitted; documented).
Predicate language for filter: field op literal (ops: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=, ~).
Unit + acceptance tests using NDJSON fixture streams under tests/fixtures/.
non_goals: Not a SQL replacement; not a query language; no JOINs across non-streaming sources.
Not a replacement for jq; jq stays the tool for ad-hoc JSON outside the agent-tool schema.
No tool-discovery or orchestration (apipe doesn't decide which upstream tool to call).
No CBOR / binary wire format in this slice (NDJSON only; CBOR is Phase 3).
No `apipe expand-evidence` / `context` / `attribute` / `match-episode` (those are Phase 1+; require integration with recall/fsstory/memlog).
Not modifying recall, transcript, fsstory, etc. to ship `--format apipe` (separate slices).

five_whys_trace.q: Why a shared record schema and a runtime binary?
five_whys_trace.a: Today every tool emits a slightly-different JSON shape; chaining them requires hand-rolled jq filters per pair. The combinatorial cost grows with each new tool. A shared schema makes chains additive: each stage enriches the same record shape rather than translating between dialects.

five_whys_trace.q: Why NDJSON and not CBOR or a binary format?
five_whys_trace.a: NDJSON is line-oriented (preserves Unix-pipe ergonomics), readable in raw form (debugging is just `head`), and supported by every existing tool that already emits structured JSON. CBOR is a Phase 3 optimization for long pipelines.

five_whys_trace.q: Why ship Phase 0 without the adapter changes to recall/transcript/etc.?
five_whys_trace.a: The runtime is the load-bearing piece. Once it exists with a stable schema, adapter PRs are mechanical (already-structured data → remap to shared schema). Shipping the runtime first prevents adapter work from racing against an unstable schema.

five_whys_trace.q: Why a predicate language (apipe filter) instead of embedding jq?
five_whys_trace.a: Phase 0 stays self-contained. jq is a runtime dep (or vendored crate); a tiny in-house predicate language is ~200 LoC and covers 80% of the filtering needed in agent pipelines. jq integration (`apipe filter -j`) is a Phase 3 add-on if needed.

five_whys_trace.q: Why bounded-memory for top/filter but unbounded for sort/pretty?
five_whys_trace.a: Sort needs all input to order it; pretty needs full table width to align columns. These are sink stages where the buffer cost is acceptable. Streaming stages (pass/top/filter/to-paths/from-paths) are the chainable backbone; sinks are the human-facing end.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should the schema be enforced strictly (reject extra fields) or permissively?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Permissive: required fields enforced, additional fields preserved through the pipeline. Strict validation is for the future `apipe check` lint stage.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What does `apipe pass` do with malformed lines?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Drops the line + writes a stderr diagnostic with line number; processing continues. Pipeline-level error visibility without aborting the chain.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should subcommands chain via a single binary or separate executables?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Single binary, multiple subcommands (`apipe pass | apipe top`). The single-binary form is simpler to ship and matches the PRD's design.
